summary: Fowler addresses the common claim that agile methods are only suitable for elite developers, acknowledging that this question is genuinely unanswerable because agile has primarily been adopted by early adopters who tend to be more skilled. He argues that while agile does require some high-ability people on a team, so do plan-driven methods—the difference is how those leaders work with the team. Drawing on Thoughtworks' experience with mixed-ability client teams, he suggests that agile's collaborative nature may actually work well across skill levels, though he admits this is speculation until more average teams try it.
key_points: New techniques are naturally tried first by higher-ability developers, making it impossible to know if they work for average teams until those teams actually try them
Both agile and plan-driven methods require some high-ability people—the difference is whether you get more leverage from plans or from collaboration
The 'individuals and interactions over processes and tools' principle means agilists expect high-ability teams to outperform regardless of methodology
Thoughtworks' mixed-ability co-sourced projects suggest agile's collaborative nature transfers well across skill levels
money_quotes: When a new technique or tool appears, it's usually tried out first by higher ability developers. This is quite a natural response.
This is an unanswerable question, because until it's tried by more average teams, you can't tell how it will work for them.
This PeopleOriented assumption means that agilists will always expect a high-ability cohesive team to do better than a team of lower ability, whichever one follows more agile approaches.
Both approaches require *some* high-ability people, although the way those leaders work with the team is different.
The open question is whether you get more leverage of the high-ability people from plans or from collaboration.
key_insight: The question of whether agile works for average developers is fundamentally unanswerable until average teams actually try it—early adoption bias makes all current evidence unreliable.
